description:
Line 337 in secretx-tpm2/src/lib.rs:

```rust
let data = MaxNvBuffer::try_from(bytes.to_vec()).map_err(|e| {
```

`bytes` is `Zeroizing<Vec<u8>>`. Calling `.to_vec()` goes through `Deref<Target=Vec<u8>>` → `AsRef<[u8]>` → slice `.to_vec()`, which allocates a new `Vec<u8>` and copies the secret bytes. The original `Zeroizing<Vec<u8>>` still holds its copy until drop.

The copy is then moved into `MaxNvBuffer` (which wraps it in its own `Zeroizing`), so both ARE zeroed on drop — the comment on lines 335-336 is accurate. But between the `.to_vec()` call and the `MaxNvBuffer` construction, two heap copies of the secret coexist. This is a security hygiene issue, not a performance one (NV data is ≤2KB, one-shot operation).

What goes wrong: Momentarily doubles secret-data footprint in memory. In a crash dump or core file, both copies would be visible. More importantly, if MaxNvBuffer::try_from returns Err (data too large), the plain Vec<u8> is dropped WITHOUT zeroing while the Zeroizing wrapper zeros its copy — one copy leaks into freed heap.

Suggested fix: Extract the inner Vec without copying:

```rust
let raw: Vec<u8> = std::mem::take(&mut *bytes);
let data = MaxNvBuffer::try_from(raw).map_err(|e| {
```

Or if `zeroize::Zeroizing` exposes `into_inner()` in the pinned version:

```rust
let data = MaxNvBuffer::try_from(bytes.into_inner()).map_err(|e| {
```
title: NV write: extra copy of secret data
